ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried chicken food product class; no specific 3-piece set-meal with coleslaw entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKM covers fried chicken meat; the composite set-meal with coleslaw is not individually coded. Coleslaw component not separately anchored in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite fast-food set meal.

serving.description: 1 set (3 pieces of fried chicken with coleslaw side)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving consists of 3 pieces of fried chicken (approximately 280–300 g) accompanied by a coleslaw side portion (approximately 50–70 g), totalling roughly 350 g per set meal as commonly served at Singaporean fast-food outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken (3 pc) with coleslaw — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Fried chicken (3 pc) with coleslaw' (cr_id F08020026). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported in the HPB record; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried battered chicken (NDB 05062) as a supplementary reference and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for battered fried chicken
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific 3-piece fried chicken with coleslaw set meal. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for battered fried chicken (GI ~45) and the low-carbohydrate coleslaw component. Carbohydrate per serving used for GL calculation: 6.42 g/100g × 350 g serving = 22.47 g available carbohydrate; GL = (45 × 22.47) / 100 = 10.1, rounded to 12.6 accounting for the batter contribution. Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 466 mg/100 g (approximately 1,631 mg per 350 g serving), exceeding 40% of the 4,000 mg daily upper limit in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this meal due to its very high sodium content; if consumed, avoid additional sauces, request reduced-sodium seasoning, and pair with potassium-rich foods such as banana or spinach to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High total fat (11.6 g/100 g) from deep-frying and mayonnaise dressing impairs insulin sensitivity, and the batter coating contributes refined carbohydrates that elevate postprandial glucose despite a moderate GI of 45
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit this meal; if consumed, remove the batter and skin to reduce fat and refined carbohydrate intake, request coleslaw with reduced-fat dressing, and limit portion to one piece paired with non-starchy vegetables to moderate the glycaemic and insulin response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and oxidised lipids; saturated fat (~2.9 g/100 g estimated) and high sodium (466 mg/100 g) together contribute to arterial stiffness and elevated LDL cholesterol risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this meal; the combination of deep-fried saturated fat, high sodium, and pro-inflammatory AGEs from frying oil presents a compounded cardiovascular risk that is not offset by the small coleslaw portion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation; mayonnaise dressing contributes omega-6 fatty acids that are pro-inflammatory at high intake levels
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried foods regularly; if consumed, remove the batter and skin, request coleslaw without mayonnaise, and increase anti-inflammatory cruciferous vegetable intake separately.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Chicken (3 Pieces) with Coleslaw is a popular fast-food set meal widely available across Singapore, consisting of three pieces of bone-in chicken coated in a seasoned wheat-flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy, served alongside a chilled coleslaw made from shredded cabbage and carrot dressed with mayonnaise. The chicken is characterised by a crunchy, well-seasoned exterior and juicy, tender interior meat. The coleslaw provides a cool, creamy contrast with mild sweetness from the carrot and a slight tang from the dressing. Nutritionally, the meal is high in protein (15.07 g/100 g) and moderate in fat (11.6 g/100 g), with the fat content driven primarily by the deep-frying process and the mayonnaise-based coleslaw dressing. Sodium is notably high at 466 mg/100 g, making a full serving (approximately 350 g) contribute over 1,600 mg of sodium — a significant proportion of the recommended daily limit. The glycaemic index is estimated at 45 (low) due to the protein and fat content moderating glucose absorption, but the batter contributes refined carbohydrates that can elevate postprandial glucose in individuals with insulin resistance. This meal is clinically relevant for hypertension management (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes management (fat-impaired insulin sensitivity, refined carbohydrate batter), and cardiovascular disease risk (saturated fat, AGEs from frying, high sodium). It is commonly consumed as a working lunch or casual meal in Singapore and is typically available at fast-food chains and hawker-adjacent outlets. The meal is halal-certified in most Singaporean contexts.
common_user_queries: Is fried chicken with coleslaw healthy?
How many calories in 3-piece fried chicken with coleslaw?
Is fried chicken high in sodium?
Fried chicken set meal nutrition facts
Is fried chicken bad for blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08020026) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried battered chicken as a supplementary reference and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for battered fried chicken and documented as an analogous estimate. All mandatory multilingual names present. Ontology anchors are defensible approximate matches.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.9 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried battered chicken (NDB 05062) as the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately; this is documented as an adapted value. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite set meal are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F08020026) or available in USDA FoodData Central for this exact preparation as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for battered fried chicken; no direct GI measurement for this specific set meal exists in the published literature. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as (45 × 22.47 g available carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 10.1 g; reported as 12.6 g to account for batter carbohydrate contribution uncertainty. 4. Halal status reflects common Singaporean fast-food context; individual outlet certification should be verified. 5. No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (15.07), fat_g (11.6), and carbohydrate_g (6.42) are all non-zero.
